text.title: Disjoint Tool Permissions for RED and GREEN
text.summary: RED can write tests but not edit sources; GREEN can edit sources but not modify tests; both invariants enforced by the sub-agent skill frontmatter, not by trust.

section.title: Permission disjunction
section.p: An agent fine-tuned on `allowed_tools` will respect the list. Splitting RED and GREEN into two skill files with disjoint tool sets converts "TDD discipline" from a guideline into a precondition the orchestrator can verify.

rule.statement: The RED sub-agent skill frontmatter MUST list only `Read` and `Write` (no `Edit`), and Write MUST be path-restricted to `tests/`. The GREEN sub-agent skill frontmatter MUST list only `Read` and `Edit` (no `Write`), and Edit MUST be path-restricted to `src/`. Neither skill may include `Bash` for file moves. Any deviation fails the gate and the cycle aborts.
rule.rationale: Tool-permission discipline is the only mechanism that prevents a single LLM context from "fixing" a failing test by mutating it; trust-based prompts ("please do not edit the test file") fail under sufficiently long sessions.

example.code: # .claude/skills/tdd-red.md frontmatter description: "Write the smallest failing test for the AC." allowed_tools: [Read, Write] write_paths: ["tests/**"]
example.note: RED cannot edit `src/` even if it tries.

example.code: description: "Write a failing test, then make it pass." allowed_tools: [Read, Write, Edit, Bash]
example.note: Single agent owns both directories; will edit the test to pass.

antipattern.description: RED skill includes `Edit` "just in case it needs to fix a typo in an existing test."
antipattern.reason: That escape hatch reopens the cheating path; a typo fix belongs in a separate refactor cycle, not inside a RED turn.

section.reference: Custom TDD workflow with Claude Code (alexop.dev).
Simon Willison: red/green TDD as agentic pattern.
